question: Explain the significance of the Church-Turing thesis in the theory of computation.
expectedPoints: It asserts that any function that is effectively computable can be computed by a Turing machine.
It provides a robust and universally accepted definition of an algorithm.
It links intuitive notions of computation (like algorithms) to a formal mathematical model (the Turing machine).

question: Define the Halting Problem and state why it is undecidable.
expectedPoints: The Halting Problem is the problem of determining, from a description of an arbitrary program and an input, whether the program will finish running or continue to run forever.
It was proven undecidable by Alan Turing using a proof by contradiction.
This proof showed that no general algorithm exists that can solve the halting problem for all possible program-input pairs.

question: What is the key difference between a recursively enumerable language and a recursive language?
expectedPoints: A recursive language is decidable; there exists a Turing machine that will halt and accept all strings in the language and halt and reject all strings not in the language.
A recursively enumerable language is only semi-decidable; a Turing machine will halt and accept all strings in the language, but may not halt for strings not in the language.
